title: Method and system for ventricular assistive device adjustment using a wearable device
abstract: There is provided herein, a method for adjusting a VAD device parameters using a wearable device placed on the patient's hand, the method includes the steps of acquiring a set of signals from sensors in the wearable device, computing at least one quality metric, and adjusting at least one VAD operational parameter so as to optimize at least the one quality metric.
claims:
Claims claimed is: A method for adjusting a VAD device parameters using a wearable device placed on the patient's hand comprising steps of: acquiring a set of signals from sensors in said wearable device; computing at least one quality metric; and adjusting at least one VAD operational parameter so as to optimize at least said one quality metric. The method according to claim 1 , wherein said VAD device is an LVAD (Left Ventricular Assistive Device). The method according to claim 1, wherein said set of sensors in said wearable device comprise at least one of the following sensors: pressure sensor placed on the radial artery, PhotoPleythysmograph (PPG) sensor, Oxygen saturation level (Sp02) sensor, Accelerometer, and Temperature sensor. The method according to claim 1, further comprising a step of acquiring signals from sensors within the VAD device. The method according to claim 1, further comprising a step of acquiring signals from sensors within an additional implanted device (e.g., defibrillator, pace-maker, cardiac resynchronization therapy- CRT, vagal stimulator). The method according to claim 4, wherein said set of sensors in VAD device comprise at least one of the following sensors: pressure sensors, rotational speed sensor, and power sensor. 7. The method according to claim 1, where said quality metric comprises at least one of the following metrics: Systolic Blood Pressure, Diastolic Blood Pressure, Mean Arterial Pressure, Heart Rate, Heart Rate Variability, Blood Pressure Variability, Activity Level or Skin Temperature, and Cardiac Output. 8. The method according to claim 6, wherein said metric is acquired continuously (e.g., noninvasive blood pressure measurement). 9. The method according to claim 1, wherein said steps are repeated periodically (i.e. closed- loop adjustment). 10. The method according to claim 1, wherein said VAD parameters being optimized includes at least the VAD rotational speed or frequency. 11. The method according to claim 1 , wherein said metric comprises a detection of valvular activity (e.g. Aortic valve opening). 12. The method according to claim 1, wherein said method also comprises slowly deflating a cuff placed on the Brachial artery from a state of total Brachial artery occlusion until pulses are detected by said sensors. 13. A method of detecting valvular activity in VAD patients using a wrist worn device containing at least one sensor comprising steps of: acquiring signals from sensors in said wearable device; computing a set of features from said signals; and running an algorithm to extract time portions in which valvular activity is present. 14. The method according to claim 13, further comprising a step of acquiring signals from sensors within the VAD device. 15. The method according to claim 12, wherein said set of sensors in said wearable device comprise at least one of the following sensors: pressure sensor placed on the radial artery, PhotoPleythysmograph (PPG) sensor, Oxygen saturation level (Sp02) sensor, Accelerometer, and Temperature sensor. 16. The method according to claim 12, wherein said of features comprises at least one of the following: Systolic rise time, Diastolic fall time, Heart Rate, Dicrotic notch position, Dicrotic notch timing, Dicrotic notch detection, a Fourier pulse coefficient, and Pulse amplitude. 17. The method according to claim 12, wherein said algorithm is derived from machine learning using one of the following methods: Random Forest, Decision Tree Learning, Support Vector Machine (SVM), Artificial Neural Network, and Deep Learning. 18. The method according to claim 12, where parameters for said algorithm are derived from machine learning on signals collected from the specific patient before device becomes operational. 19. Method of computing at least one VAD implanted patient hemodynamic parameter using a wrist worn device containing at least one sensor comprising steps of: acquiring signals from sensors in said wearable device; computing a set of features from said signals; and running an algorithm to compute said hemodynamic parameter. 20. The method according to claim 18, wherein said hemodynamic parameter is either Systolic Blood Pressure, Diastolic Blood Pressure, Mean Arterial Pressure, Heart Rate, Heart Rate Variability or Systolic Pulmonary Pressure, Diastolic Pulmonary Pressure, Mean 

 Pulmonary Pressure, Pulmonary Capilary Wedge Pressure or Left Ventricular, and Diastolic Pressure. 21. The method according to claim 18, further comprising a step of acquiring signals from sensors within the VAD device. 22. The method according to claim 19, further comprising a step of acquiring signals from sensors within other implanted devices (e.g. cardio-MEMs, defibrillator, pace-maker, cardiac resynchronization therapy- CRT or vagal stimulator). 23. The method according to claim 19, wherein said set of sensors in said wearable device comprise at least one of the following sensors: pressure sensor placed on the radial artery, PhotoPleythysmograph (PPG) sensor, Oxygen saturation level (Sp02) sensor, Accelerometer, and Temperature sensor. 24. The method according to claim 19, wherein said of features comprises at least one of the following: Systolic rise time, Diastolic fall time, Heart Rate, Dicrotic notch position, Dicrotic notch timing, Dicrotic notch detection, a Fourier pulse coefficient, and Pulse amplitude. 25. The method according to claim 19, wherein said algorithm is derived from machine learning using one of the following methods: Random Forest, Decision Tree Learning, Support Vector Machine (SVM), Artificial Neural Network, and Deep Learning. 26. The method according to claim 19, where parameters for said algorithm are derived from machine learning on signals collected from the specific patient before device becomes operational. 27. The method according to claim 19, where the method further computes a risk classification based on said features or said hemodynamic parameters. 28. The method according to claim 26, where risk classification is related to at least one of: Stroke (Ischemic or hemmoragic), RV failure, Ventricular Arrythmeia, Super Ventricular Arrythmia, Hypo or Hyper- Volemia, and Aortic insufficiency. 29. The method according to claim 26, where risk classification algorithm is derived from machine learning using one of the following methods: Random Forest, Decision Tree Learning, Support Vector Machine (SVM), Artificial Neural Network, and Deep Learning.
